headline: HOGS LEAD CHARGE AS GRAINS STABILIZE
subheadline: Lean hogs added 1.8% while corn and beans found footing after Memorial liquidation.
lead: Lean hogs ran to $101.50, up 1.8% in the session's biggest livestock move, as Class III milk followed with a 3.1% overnight move that held through the close at $17.25. The grain complex found its feet after five straight days of fund liquidation, corn adding 3 cents to $4.41 and beans firming 3½ cents to $11.72. Yesterday's methodical selling pressure took a breather, but volume stayed thin and the funds haven't committed new length.
the_takeaway: Livestock leads while grains pause the liquidation, but funds still sidelined.
teaser: Hogs run 1.8% as grains find footing after Memorial fund exit.

one_number.context: Milk futures ran overnight on technical buying after breaking resistance, then held gains through the session close. The move signals dairy's seasonal strength heading into peak demand months, with butter production paying premium margins. Class IV strength is pulling the complex higher as summer cooling demand builds.

yesterdays_call.summary: Called ongoing grain liquidation as methodical fund exit, not selling pressure.
yesterdays_call.note: Grain volume stayed thin today with funds still out, exactly the methodical exit pattern predicted.

sections.title: LIVESTOCK LEADS HIGHER
sections.body: **Lean hogs broke the livestock stalemate**, adding $1.84 to $101.50 on technical buying after three days of sideways drift. Class III milk led overnight with a 3.1% move that held through the session, closing $17.25 as **butter margins stay premium** heading into peak cooling season. Live cattle slipped 40 cents to $239.80 but held above Monday's $238 test, while feeders gave back $2.10 to $348.90. The **livestock complex is pricing summer demand** while grains mark time.
sections.catalyst: Class III milk technical breakout overnight, butter production margins staying premium
sections.bottom_line: Hogs and milk lead livestock higher as summer demand patterns emerge.
sections.vs_yesterday: Livestock: broke sideways drift exactly as thin volume suggested it would

sections.title: GRAINS PAUSE LIQUIDATION
sections.body: Corn firmed 1½ cents to $4.41 and beans added 3½ cents to $11.72, the first meaningful bounce since Memorial Day fund liquidation began. **Volume stayed thin**, managed money still sidelined after five straight sessions of selling. Chicago wheat gained 6½ cents to $6.06 on technical buying, but the move lacks conviction with **no new weather premium** building despite scattered Plains showers. Deputy Ag Secretary Vaden's comments on China soybean orders provided a headline but no fresh tonnage.
sections.catalyst: USDA's Vaden expects China to honor soybean purchase commitments this year
sections.bottom_line: First bounce since Memorial liquidation, but funds haven't committed new money.

sections.title: CRUDE HOLDS IRAN GAINS
sections.body: WTI crude added $1.54 to $96.20, holding most of Monday's 5% Iran escalation premium as **overnight strikes on Kuwait airport** kept geopolitical risk bid alive. Natural gas eased a penny to $3.16 as domestic supply concerns fade with seasonal demand still weeks away. The **crude complex is pricing convoy risk** through the Persian Gulf shipping lanes, not fundamental supply disruption. Iran tensions, ongoing since early April, shifted from Strait of Hormuz threats to direct strikes on Gulf state infrastructure.
sections.catalyst: Iranian drone strikes on Kuwait International Airport overnight, escalating regional tensions
sections.bottom_line: Crude holds Iran premium as Gulf tensions escalate beyond shipping lanes.

outside_the_pit.title: North Dakota cattlemen seek CME activity investigation
outside_the_pit.body: The state's cattle group asked Justice and USDA to probe commodity futures trading patterns, claiming insurance programs have become liabilities for producers. When risk management tools work against the user, the market structure needs scrutiny.

outside_the_pit.title: New World Screwworm found 31 miles from Texas border
outside_the_pit.body: The parasitic fly that eats livestock alive surfaced in Mexico, close enough to threaten US herds. USDA Secretary Rollins dismissed immediate border concerns, but the economic damage potential runs to millions if it crosses.

outside_the_pit.title: Japan commits $19.4 billion to energy crisis cushion
outside_the_pit.body: Tokyo will spend to shield households from Middle East oil disruption inflation. The package signals how global energy markets are pricing regional conflict into structural costs, not just spot premiums.

spread_to_watch.label: July corn / December corn carry
spread_to_watch.level: 27 cents inverse, narrowing from 31 cents Monday
spread_to_watch.commentary: The inversion is shrinking as new crop December gains on old crop July, signaling the market expects normal weather through pollination. When the carry flips positive, weather premium season is over and harvest pressure pricing begins.

basis.headline: Eastern Corn Belt firming on ethanol restart demand
basis.body: **Illinois and Indiana elevators** are adding 2-4 cents to posted bids as ethanol plants restart after maintenance shutdowns. Western Belt staying soft with farmer selling light ahead of June tax deadlines. The **regional split** reflects logistics more than fundamental corn demand, but eastern producers with old-crop storage have a window the futures board isn't pricing.

weekly_thread.question: Will the grain liquidation finish before weather takes control of the story?
weekly_thread.status_text: Liquidation paused today but volume stayed thin, suggesting funds are waiting not buying. Weather season starts with pollination in three weeks.

the_more_you_know.title: Why thin volume tells the liquidation story better than price
the_more_you_know.body: Today's 3-cent corn bounce came on volume 40% below the 20-day average, typical of a pause not a reversal. Real fund re-entry shows up as volume gains before price moves, as position sizes matter more than direction to managed money. When the selling resumes, look for volume to confirm it. When buying starts, volume will lead price by at least a session.

watch_list.desc: Weekly export sales; soybeans under 300K MT keeps technical selling alive
USDA Crop Progress; corn above 45% planted removes weather premium catalyst
Live cattle above $242 tests whether yesterday's bounce has conviction

daily_quote.text: Optimism is the faith that leads to achievement. Nothing can be done without hope and confidence.

source_summary: CME/CBOT settlement data, USDA reports, industry news
